TITLE: WH2 precode candidates — untuned Pareto screen
SUBTITLE: Expected decoding overhead against encoder throughput for 84 precode configurations, each measured on the same sealed sample of 24,390 cells and faceted across six K regions. Lower is better on y, further right is better on x, so the lower-right corner is the target.
READNOTE: Every arm is untuned: one uniform construction seed at every K, exactly one construction attempt, no per-K seed tables and no seed-attempt rescues. This isolates the architecture from the seed search, which is why Wirehair 1 sits so high on y — that position is construction failure, not decoding cost.
TILENOTE: Both figures come from the same quiet single-host re-time, so the comparison is like-for-like. On this baseline no WH2 arm beats Wirehair 1 anywhere; the gap closes from roughly half at small K to near parity at K 20,000–64,000. Earlier readings above 100% were computed on the superseded 24-worker loki pass and did not survive re-baselining.
FINDINGS: No WH2 arm beats Wirehair 1 on encoder throughput in any region. The fastest WH2 arm per region reaches 52 / 46 / 65 / 77 / 81 / 96 percent of WH1 across r1–r6. The 130 percent recorded at r6 was computed on the superseded 24-worker loki pass, which measured Wirehair 1 at 2.19M sym/s; the uniform 4-worker quiet re-time puts it at 2.84M in the same region and the advantage disappears. Run contention is the documented mechanism for exactly this shape of error — the discarded 24-worker foureyes pass depressed bandwidth-bound r6 medians by up to 25 percent — though that diagnosis is inferred for the loki pass rather than measured.

The throughput envelope has no crossovers left. After the tiny-K fast path landed, the equal-block-size re-measurement is won by mixed-p244-d4 essentially everywhere, and the former small-K identity-corner regimes are eliminated rather than narrowed. That is why the K-dispatch table collapses to a single band: dispatch profile v1 is [2, 64000] → mixed-p244-frozen-10x2-d4.

The dispatch wrapper adds no decoding behaviour of its own. wh2-dispatched-v1 and mixed-p244-d4 report identical expected overhead in all six regions (0.0192 / 0.0011 / 0 / 0 / 0 / 0.0013) and identical dead fractions — they are the same precode entered two ways.

Those two identical arms disagree on x by up to 7.6 percent. Because they are the same configuration, that spread is the measurement floor rather than a difference. Three further groups of arms whose per-cell outcomes are byte-identical in all six regions spread by 6.3, 6.8 and 20.2 percent, each worst in r1. Treat horizontal gaps below roughly 8 percent — and below 20 percent in r1 — as unresolvable.

The all-GF(256) hypothesis stays refuted. The certified arms are slower than the mixed-field arms in every region (region medians 1.88 / 2.63 / 2.39 / 2.38 / 2.07 / 1.23 M sym/s versus 3.09 / 3.19 / 3.17 / 3.23 / 2.98 / 2.23 M), and they are timed at a smaller block, which if anything flatters them on a symbols-per-second axis.

Wirehair 1's y position is constructability, not decode margin. It is the only arm in the grid with construction failures — 538 across 24,390 cells — and those censored cells supply 81–90 percent of its expected overhead in every region. Excluding them, its mean extra is only 0.015–0.021. Per-K seed tables buy WH1 constructability; this screen deliberately removes them.

Small K was a fixed-init-cost problem, not a per-symbol-arithmetic one. In the round-2 micro-timing at K = 8, encoder init is 11,136 ns of an 11,156 ns total for the best WH2 combo versus 1,471 ns of 1,512 ns for WH1, while emitting all K systematic symbols costs 20 ns and 40 ns respectively. That is what motivated the fast path, and it is where the remaining small-K gap still lives.

The fast-path seam is clean. At the auto gate K = 512 → 513 with two-byte blocks, outcomes are identical with the fast path forced on and off across K = 448–576, every seam K decodes at overhead 0 on the probe stream, and the timing step is a bounded 21.3 percent with no overhead anomaly.
PANELS:
All 84 arms run untuned: a single uniform u64 construction seed at every K, exactly one construction attempt, no per-K seed tables and no seed-attempt rescues. The sample is stratified, sealed and shared byte-for-byte by every arm — sample seed 0x6789cafe (SplitMix64), manifest schema v4, sealed 2026-07-23T21:19:16Z. Per region: r1 [2,100] 99 K × 10 construction seeds = 990 cells, r2 900 × 5 = 4,500, r3 400 × 3 = 1,200, r4 500 × 3 = 1,500, r5 1,000 × 3 = 3,000, r6 4,400 × 3 = 13,200 — 24,390 cells per arm, plus 24 timing K per region. Losses are iid at 10 percent on structure-only zero-byte trials with a paired overhead stream.

y — expected overhead. The mean, over the region's cells, of the exact number of extra symbols needed for first successful decode, found by an ascending scan with early stop and censored at +4 absolute extras. A cell that never decodes inside the cap contributes exactly 4, so the metric is a bounded charge rather than an unbounded tail. Points at exact zero sit in the band below the log axis.

x — encoder throughput. Encoder initialization plus emission of the first K systematic symbols and nothing else: no repair symbols, no decoding. Minimal block size for the family, single-threaded, reported as the median symbols per second over the region's 24 timing K at 9 reps each.
Timing baseline — what changed since the previous plot

The x-axis of this page is not the x-axis of the previous one. That plot used timing_quiet_summary.json: 83 arms, host loki, 24 workers, measured before the tiny-K fast path existed. It is superseded.

The canonical baseline is now timing_quiet_foureyes_fastpath.json — 84 arms × 6 regions, host foureyes, 4 workers, 9 reps, a full-grid timing-only re-time on the tiny-K fast path at merge 00fcc960. Cross-host comparability rests on byte-identical binaries: both build byte-identical on loki and foureyes (same gcc 13.3.0, same Threadripper PRO 9985WX, MARCH_NATIVE off). The box was idle — k10temp Tctl 33.2 °C before, 79.8 °C peak, 38.0 °C at post-run idle.

A first 24-worker pass was run and discarded: its start burst launched all large-K tasks concurrently and depressed bandwidth-bound rates unevenly, up to about 25 percent on the r6 median, which would have read as a codec difference. The accepted 4-worker pass is uniform — 505 tasks, 181.7 s wall, 0 failed.

Scale of the change, using WH1 as the control (the fast path does not touch it, so its new/old ratio is pure measurement environment — host, worker count and run conditions together): WH1 gains 9 / 9 / 12 / 10 / 15 / 29 percent across r1–r6, while the WH2 arms gain a median of 74 percent at r1 (max 2.95×) and 9–26 percent elsewhere. Essentially all of the small-K movement is the fast path; essentially all of the large-K movement is the host.

• r1 is not scored on identical cells. The protocol requires the same cells for every arm and r2–r6 satisfy it exactly, but 12 identity-corner arms cannot construct the smallest K by design and drop them: ic-d6-p244 omits K=2 (980 cells), four ic-d8-* arms omit K=2–4 (960), and seven more omit K=2–5 (950). Since the dropped K are the hardest in the region, those arms are scored on an easier denominator. Use the r1: like-for-like toggle to put all 84 arms on the common K ≥ 6 subset — on that basis wh2-dispatched-v1 falls from 0.0192 to 0.0053 while ic-d6-p244 barely moves, so the published r1 figures overstate the identity-corner margin by roughly 3.6×.
  • The x-axis is a narrow metric. Encoder init plus the first K systematic symbols only. It excludes repair-symbol generation entirely and decoding entirely, so arms whose cost is paid at systematic-symbol time but repaid at repair time — the mixing arms are the flagged example — are systematically undervalued.
  • Block size is structural and differs by family. Minimum block bytes are 1 for WH1 and the certified arms, 2 for the mixed arms. The axis is symbols per second, not bytes per second, so a mixed arm already moves twice the payload per symbol. Ranking at large payloads, where the encoder is memory-bandwidth-bound rather than setup-bound, may differ.
  • x and y do not share a seed regime. Overhead uses the fixed untuned construction seed; timing uses production seed derivation with the tuning knobs applied.
  • The timing inside the result JSONs is not what is plotted. Round-1 result files carry their own contended 60-worker timing and round-2 files carry loki pre-fast-path timing. Only the canonical quiet re-time feeds this x-axis.
  • Host and code changed together between the previous plot and this one — loki to foureyes, and pre- to post-fast-path. They separate only through the WH1 control.
  • The mechanism data behind the small-K finding is pre-fast-path. It explains why the fast path was built; it does not describe the encoder that produced this x-axis.
  • The +4 cap censors the tail. Any cell needing more than 4 extra symbols is charged exactly 4, so expected overhead understates true tail cost. The dead, fail@+0 and fail@+2 fractions carried on every point are the honest tail reporting.
  • One draw per cell. One trial and one construction attempt per cell, so only the region aggregate is meaningful.
  • In r1, x and y cover different K. r1 overhead spans K = 2–100 but r1 timing samples K = 6–90, so the two axes are not measured over the same K set in that facet.
FOOTER: 84 configurations × 6 regions = 504 points · 24,390 sealed cells per configuration · sample seed 0x6789cafe, manifest schema v4 sealed 2026-07-23T21:19:16Z · cells: round 1 (loki r1–r4, foureyes r5–r6), round 2 (loki, all regions), dispatch and fast path round 3 at 0ff1f38 · plotted timing: full-grid re-time at 00fcc960 on idle foureyes, 4 workers, 9 reps, superseding the loki pre-fast-path timing in timing_quiet_summary.json · per-cell distributions, fail@+0/+2, dead and hard-K fractions live in bench/results/untuned_pareto_screen_v1 and _v2 on branch exp/wh2-untuned-pareto · rendered by bench/render_untuned_pareto_html.py.
